narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and family background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the lowest 10th percentile (far below typical students' mid-range positions around the 5th or 6th decile), low family income bracket, LSAT score of 33 (under the average of about 37), undergraduate GPA of 3.10 (slightly below the 3.21 average), and full-time student status—and predicted a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This close call tipped over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom 10%, which strongly signaled to the model a significant drop or stagnation in performance compared to peers who typically improve or hold steady in higher deciles. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile at the bottom 10% reinforced this concern, suggesting early academic struggles that carried forward and deviated from the stronger starts most successful students have. On a more positive note, your low family income actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing, as the model recognizes that students from similar backgrounds often show resilience and outperform expectations. Your LSAT, GPA, and full-time status played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who boast higher decile rankings and stronger test scores, your early and sustained low class standings stood out as the biggest red flags, even with your solid undergraduate record. This doesn't define your potential—many from modest backgrounds like yours have turned things around. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, seeking academic advising to improve your decile rankings in any ongoing studies, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your determination as an Asian male navigating these challenges. Building a narrative of upward momentum through additional coursework or internships could also strengthen future applications to other programs. With targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next cycle.
